title: GWAM Enterprise Engagement Program — Recognizing Efficiency at Scale
situation: Global Wealth & Asset Management was looking to embed a culture of continuous efficiency and to recognize teams that proactively found and delivered improvements. There was no enterprise-wide mechanism to surface, validate, and celebrate efficiency contributions across the organization, and the absence of one was masking real value being created at the team level.
obstacle: Programs of this type can become low-impact recognition exercises if they are not anchored to real measurement. Building credibility required a consistent way to validate efficiency claims, translate them into hard outcomes (FTE equivalents, cost savings, environmental impact), and surface them in a way that genuinely engaged employees rather than feeling performative.
actions: Led the GWAM enterprise-wide employee engagement program for one year, focused on recognizing teams that increased operational efficiency
Designed the validation, measurement, and recognition mechanism for efficiency contributions
Translated team-level improvements into enterprise outcomes including FTE equivalents, cost savings, and environmental impact
Engaged employees across regions and business lines, building visibility for the contributions teams were making
results: Saved the equivalent of approximately 11 FTEs through validated efficiency improvements
Generated more than $2M per year in annualized savings
Equivalent environmental impact of preserving roughly 20 trees per year
Strengthened culture of continuous improvement and recognition across GWAM
fullStory: I ran the GWAM enterprise efficiency engagement program for a year. The point was to surface, validate, and recognize the work teams were doing to improve efficiency, then aggregate it into something the organization could see and celebrate. The mechanics mattered: without rigorous validation, the program would have devolved into recognition theater. With it, we ended up demonstrating that the teams had collectively saved the equivalent of around 11 FTEs, more than $2M per year, and an environmental impact roughly equivalent to 20 trees per year. The recognition piece worked because the underlying numbers were real.
